steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Build a React single-page Hashi (Bridges) puzzle game with a retro terminal look: black background, green monospace text, no CSS frameworks. Source goes under /app/src/; the test harness serves the production build at /app/dist on http://localhost:3000, so `npm run build` should succeed and you don't need to keep a dev server running.

clicking island A then island B cycles the bridge between them through none → single → double → none. Right-click or Ctrl+click on an existing bridge removes it entirely (single or double, both go to none) — the click target is the bridge `<line>` element itself (anywhere along the line, including its midpoint), not the island endpoints; attach the `onContextMenu` and Ctrl+click handlers to each rendered `<line>`, and call `e.preventDefault()` on the contextmenu event so the browser menu doesn't swallow it. For a double bridge that means both parallel `<line>`s individually carry the handlers (a single handler on a wrapping `<g>` is not sufficient — the click must register when targeted directly on either line), and removing either one clears the pair. Arrow keys move a visible cursor between islands; Enter selects an island, arrow keys move toward another, Enter toggles the bridge; Esc cancels. The cursor is parked on the first island (id 0) from page load and after any puzzle change, and the key listeners live on window/document — not on a focused DOM node — so a fresh page that has just been blurred still reacts to Enter without needing a prior arrow press. Invalid moves render a descriptive error inside an element with class error-message, cleared on the next valid move — and "cleared on the next valid move" means: when the second click of a pair produces an error, also reset the in-progress selection in the same state update, so the very next click starts a fresh A→B pair that runs the toggle (do not let the post-error click be interpreted as a deselect). The error-message element must be removed (or hidden) the moment a subsequent valid bridge toggle executes. The error text must be one of: "Cannot connect non-adjacent islands", "Max 2 bridges per pair", "Island would exceed its number", "Bridges would cross".

The board is a single SVG of 800×600 anchored to the viewport top-left — no centering, no page padding. Each grid unit is 40px, so an island at grid (x, y) renders at pixel (x*40, y*40). Islands are SVG circle elements; bridges are line elements (two parallel lines for a double). The keyboard cursor must be clearly visible but must not be a circle — use rect, stroke filter, group transform, anything else — so the board's circle count always equals the island count.

Islands are numbered circles with values 1–8; the number is the exact bridge count required at that island. Bridges are straight horizontal or vertical lines between two islands, max two per pair, never crossing. The puzzle is solved when every island's count is satisfied and all islands form one connected component.

Puzzle data lives at /app/src/data/puzzles.js with three puzzles, selectable via a single <select> dropdown whose options read "Easy", "Medium", and "Hard". Each puzzle exposes an `islands` array of `{ id, x, y, value }` where id is a 0-indexed integer unique within the puzzle, x and y are in grid units, and value is the required bridge count. Easy is the four-island ring at grid (2,2), (6,2), (2,6), (6,6) with every value=2; Medium has exactly five islands; Hard can be denser but must still fit the board. A Reset button clears all bridges on the current puzzle while leaving the islands in place. When the puzzle is solved, render a banner with class victory-banner containing the text SOLVED!.

One thing worth flagging from prototyping: the error-message and victory-banner only need to exist while their condition is active, and the dropdown handler should fully reset bridges when the puzzle changes — clearing the bridges map (and any selection/error/victory state) in the same state update that swaps the puzzle, so the next render shows zero `<line>` elements. The first mouse click on an island is just selection — the toggle runs on the second click against a different island, and once that toggle fires the selection clears so the next click starts a fresh pair. Resist the urge to layer invisible or wider `<line>` elements over the visible bridge for easier click detection: the total `<line>` count is observable and should equal the visible bridge count (one per single, two per double).

Game logic lives at /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js. Bridges are stored as Map<string, number> keyed by the canonical "x1,y1,x2,y2" string where the endpoint with the smaller (x, then y) sorts first; coordinates in the key are grid units. canAddBridge(islands, bridges, from, to, delta) returning { valid, error } where error, when non-null, is one of the four strings above; computeCurrentCounts(islands, bridges) returning Map<islandId, number> keyed by the island's numeric id (handle id=0 carefully); checkConnectivity(islands, bridges) returning whether every island is reachable from every other; isVictory(islands, bridges) returning whether every island's count equals its value, no bridges cross, and the graph is connected.

canAddBridge should also guard the degenerate from === to case (valid: false; the error string there is unconstrained).



Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@6c315548159e:/app#

Analysis: We are at /app with no files inspected yet. The task requires creating a React SPA Hashi puzzle with specific SVG rendering, keyboard and mouse interactions, puzzle data, and build output to /app/dist via npm run build. We need to inspect the existing project structure (package.json, src layout) and then implement /app/src/data/puzzles.js, /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js, and the React app under /app/src/ with required UI/behavior, then run tests/build.
Plan: 1) Inspect repository structure and existing React tooling (Vite/webpack) by listing files and reading package.json.
2) Inspect current /app/src contents to see what exists.
3) Implement puzzles.js with 3 puzzles (Easy/Medium/Hard) matching requirements.
4) Implement hashiLogic.js with keying, counts, adjacency/crossing checks, connectivity, and victory.
5) Implement React UI: single 800x600 SVG at top-left, terminal styling, islands as circles+text, cursor as non-circle (rect), bridge lines with handlers on each line (including both for double), selection logic, error-message/victory-banner conditional rendering, dropdown and reset behavior, keyboard navigation with global listeners.
6) Run npm install (if needed) and npm run build, then fix any issues.

steps.message:
Analysis: This is a Vite+React project. src/ already exists with App.jsx, index.jsx, index.css, and src/data/puzzles.js (currently likely placeholder). There is no src/utils/hashiLogic.js yet. We need to implement the full game per spec: puzzles.js with 3 puzzles, hashiLogic utilities, and update App.jsx/index.css accordingly. Also ensure Vite build outputs to /app/dist (default is dist) and npm run build succeeds.
Plan: 1) Inspect current contents of App.jsx, puzzles.js, index.jsx, index.css.
2) Create src/utils/hashiLogic.js implementing bridge keying, counts, adjacency/crossing, connectivity, victory.
3) Replace App.jsx with full UI and interaction logic: SVG board, island selection/toggling, per-line handlers for removal, keyboard cursor+selection, error-message and victory-banner conditional, dropdown and reset that fully clears state.
4) Update index.css to enforce retro terminal look and ensure SVG is anchored top-left with no padding/margins.
5) Run npm run build to validate.

Analysis: App.jsx is a placeholder. puzzles.js already has 3 puzzles with correct Easy layout and Medium has 5 islands; Hard has 6 islands and fits board. We still need all game logic and UI behavior: SVG rendering, bridge state as Map keyed by canonical coords, validation (adjacent/no-cross/max2/island count), connectivity/victory, mouse interactions including per-line contextmenu/Ctrl+click removal, keyboard cursor navigation and selection, dropdown+reset, and conditional error/victory elements. Also need index.css tweaks to ensure SVG anchored top-left and retro look across UI controls.
Plan: 1) Implement src/utils/hashiLogic.js with required exported functions: key helpers, computeCurrentCounts, crossing detection, canAddBridge, checkConnectivity, isVictory.
2) Update src/data/puzzles.js slightly to ensure select options are exactly Easy/Medium/Hard (already) and export shape expected.
3) Replace App.jsx with full game: manage puzzle selection, bridges Map, selectedIslandId, cursorIslandId, error, victory; render controls (select+reset) and the 800x600 SVG with islands, bridges, and cursor rect. Implement click logic for islands (first selects, second toggles with validation; errors reset selection in same update; valid move clears error). Implement per-line handlers for removal that remove the entire pair for that edge. Add window keydown listener to handle arrows/Enter/Escape and ensure cursor resets to island 0 on puzzle change and after any puzzle change/reset.
4) Expand index.css for terminal UI and remove any padding/centering; ensure SVG is at top-left.
5) Run npm run build.

Analysis: Production build succeeds. Core files were added/updated: src/utils/hashiLogic.js, src/App.jsx, and styling in src/index.css. Remaining risk is spec compliance details: (1) right-click/Ctrl+click removal must be on each rendered <line> (done). (2) error clearing on next valid move and selection reset on error (done). (3) adjacency should mean nearest visible island in that direction with no island between (implemented by clear-line scan). (4) crossing detection (implemented). (5) cursor resets to island 0 after any puzzle change (done) and after any bridge toggle/remove/reset (done). (6) key listeners on window and work without focus (done). One potential issue: canAddBridge crossing check currently checks existing bridges but not candidate itself vs others when there is already a bridge in that pair (key matches); that’s fine since it skips same key. Another potential issue: remove by Ctrl+click requires clicking line; we only remove if ctrlKey onClick; ok. We should quickly sanity-check exports and ensure puzzles.js matches required shape (it does). No further terminal actions required unless we want to lint/preview.
Plan: Run a quick grep to ensure required class names exist (error-message, victory-banner) and that the SVG is 800x600 and uses circles only for islands (cursor is rect). Then we can mark task complete.

Analysis: Build artifacts exist in /app/dist and the required UI classnames and SVG sizing are present in src/App.jsx. The cursor is rendered as a <rect>, so island circle count equals island count. No further changes are indicated by the terminal output; project is ready for grading.
Plan: No further commands needed. Mark task complete for grading.
